data.title: Bladex, Silver Birch formalize strategic alliance to expand in Latin America
data.description:
Panama-based Banco Latinoamericano de Comercio Exterior (NYSE:BLX) and the international advisory firm Silver Birch Finance formalized a strategic alliance with a view to expand access to working capital financing for corporates in Latin America.

The alliance highlights Bladex's regional track record and institutional strength and Silver Birch's specialized structuring and execution capabilities.

Bladex and Silver Birch have successfully completed programs with clients in various sectors in countries such as Colombia, Guatemala, Mexico, Panama, Peru, Puerto Rico and the Dominican Republic.

data.title: Burlington Stores reaffirms flat to 2% comp sales growth guidance for 2025 amid tariff volatility and consumer uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Burlington Stores (BURL) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael O’Sullivan opened by noting, "Total sales grew by 6% on top of 11% growth last year. Meanwhile, our comp sales were flat on top of 2% comp growth last year. Both metrics were at the midpoint of our guidance." He highlighted that EBIT margin increased 30 basis points and adjusted EPS rose 18% year over year, attributing the performance to timing favorability and aggressive margin and expense savings in anticipation of tariff pressures.
  • O’Sullivan reiterated, "Our full year comp sales and earnings guidance has not changed from the outlook we shared in early March. We feel good about our ability to achieve this guidance, but we recognize that the level of external uncertainty has increased over the last couple of months."
  • He addressed the impact of tariffs, stating, "The tariffs that were announced in early April were of scope and a scale well beyond the expectations of most analysts... This stop-start surge volatility is likely to lead to shortages in some merchandise categories, but it might also create excess supply in others. We see both risks and opportunities in the months ahead, and we are managing our business accordingly."
  • O’Sullivan also pointed to longer-term strategies: "Our sales guidance for 2025 is predicated on opening 100 net new stores this year. We remain very confident in our ability to hit this number of openings."
  • CFO Kristin Wolfe reported, "Total sales grew 6% while comp store sales were flat, which was at the midpoint of our guidance range. The gross margin rate for the first quarter was 43.8%, an increase of 30 basis points versus last year."
  • Wolfe detailed, "Our adjusted earnings per share in Q1 was $1.67, which came in above our guidance range. This represents an 18% increase versus the prior year."
  • Wolfe confirmed, "We ended the quarter with approximately $1.1 billion in total liquidity," and highlighted a new two-year $500 million share repurchase authorization.

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed, "Our comp guidance for Q2 and for the full year 2025 is flat to up 2%." Wolfe added, "For 2025, we still expect total sales growth in the range of 6% to 8%. This assumes 100 net new store openings this year."
  • Wolfe outlined, "We are still forecasting comp store sales to increase in the range of flat to 2%, and our adjusted EBIT margin to be in the range of flat to an increase of 30 basis points versus last year. All of this results in adjusted earnings per share guidance in the range of $8.70 to $9.30. This is consistent with our initial FY ‘25 guidance."
  • The guidance is contingent on tariffs not increasing, modest inflationary impacts, and no material increase in ocean freight costs above contracted rates.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBIT margin was 6.1%, up 30 basis points from the prior year, and above guidance. Gross margin rate reached 43.8%, up 30 basis points, driven by merchandise margin and lower freight expense.
  • Product sourcing costs increased to $197 million from $183 million in Q1 2024.
  • Wolfe stated, "At the end of the quarter, comparable SOAR inventories were down 8% versus the end of the first quarter of 2024. Our reserve inventory was 48% of our total inventory versus 40% of our inventory last year."
  • The company bought $105 million in stock during the quarter, with $158 million remaining under its existing repurchase authorization.
  • Seven net new stores were opened, increasing the store count to 1,115.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JP Morgan: Asked if tariffs were good or bad for off-price retailers and for reactions to recent court rulings. O’Sullivan replied, "It feels like another curve ball that demonstrates the importance of being flexible and nimble, but candidly, we don’t yet know what it means."
  • Ike Boruchow, Wells Fargo: Asked about monthly comp sales trends and May performance. Wolfe responded, "Our comp growth for the March-April combined period was up 1%. So this is certainly better than February, but we would like to be doing better than that. And as for the last part of your question, May months to date, the trend in May has been fairly similar to that of the March and April combined trend at about the middle of our Q2 guidance range."
  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America: Inquired about demographic trends. O’Sullivan said, "Our slowdown in comp growth from Q4 to Q1 was fairly broad based affecting stores in all demographic trade areas... their comp sales trend continued to be stronger than the chain as a whole."
  • John Kernan, TD Cowen: Sought clarification on guidance risk. O’Sullivan identified the macro economy and inflation as the main risks, stating, "Our focus and our strategy are to find ways to offer great value to customers."
  • Alex Straton, Morgan Stanley: Asked about regional and category performance. Wolfe noted, "The Southeast region was -- outperformed the chain, was above the chain, while the Midwest region trailed the chain. The best-performing category in the quarter was our beauty business."
  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Asked for a marketing update. O’Sullivan described, "Our marketing message is now focused on one thing: value... If you walk into one of our new or recently remodeled stores, you will see an environment that’s very different to historic Burlington."
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Queried timing of store openings. Wolfe answered, "We expect about 25% of stores to open in the first half of the year, and 75% or the majority will be opening in the second half, but those will almost all be in the third quarter in 2025."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on the impact of tariffs and guidance resilience, showing a slightly negative to neutral tone, particularly around the uncertainty of tariffs and macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing flexibility and readiness to manage volatility. In the Q&A, O’Sullivan was measured and occasionally cautious, using phrases such as "we don’t yet know what it means" and "we are managing our business accordingly."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased caution, particularly regarding tariffs and consumer trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s comp sales guidance and EPS outlook for 2025 remain unchanged quarter over quarter, despite increased uncertainty from tariffs and macroeconomic conditions.
  • In Q4 2024, management was more optimistic about consumer trends and market share gains, while in Q1 2025, the focus shifted more to risk management and flexibility amid external volatility.
  • Analysts this quarter focused more heavily on downside risks and sought specifics on mitigation strategies, while previously their questions centered more on expansion, inventory, and competitive positioning.
  • Management’s confidence in operational execution and adaptability remained consistent, though current commentary placed greater emphasis on navigating short-term disruptions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Key risks discussed were the impact of tariffs, macroeconomic slowdown, and the sensitivity of core customers to inflation and changes in discretionary income.
  • Management mitigation strategies include aggressive margin and expense management, diversifying sourcing, vendor collaboration, and reserve inventory build-up to offset cost pressures.
  • Wolfe specifically cited guidance assumptions: tariff rates remaining at 30% for China and 10% for other countries, modest industry inflation, and no material increase in ocean freight above contracted rates as critical to the outlook.

Final Takeaway

Burlington Stores reaffirmed its 2025 guidance for total sales growth and comp sales despite rising external uncertainty, particularly from tariffs and the state of the consumer. Management underscored its readiness to adapt quickly to changing conditions and highlighted ongoing investments in merchandising, marketing, and new store expansion as levers to drive future growth while actively managing risk and protecting margins.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Synopsys suspends Q3, full-year guidance after China export controls surface
data.description:
Just one day after Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) released its second quarter financial results and guidance, the company has suspended its guidance for the quarter in progress and full-year fiscal 2025 due to new export controls related to China.

"On May 29, 2025, following the announcement of Synopsys' financial results of its second fiscal quarter ended April 30, 2025, Synopsys received a letter from the Bureau of Industry and Security of the U.S. Department of Commerce informing Synopsys of new export restrictions related to China," the company said. "Synopsys is currently assessing the potential impact of the BIS Letter on its business, operating results and financial condition."

Synposys was down 2% during afternoon trading on Thursday.  

It was revealed on Wednesday, that the BIS was sending letters to electronic design automation companies directing them to stop selling hardware, software and services to China, in an effort to slow China's development of advanced semiconductors.  The Commerce Department said it is continuing to review any U.S. exports which might be of "strategic significance" to China.

Other companies affected by the new controls include Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS), Keysight Technologies (NYSE:KEYS) and Ansys (NASDAQ:ANSS), which is currently in the process of being acquired by Synposys.

"Our pending acquisition of Ansys will address the need for new AI-powered silicon to system design solutions, integrating electronics and multi-physics," said Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi, during Wednesday night's earnings call. "We have regulatory clearances in all jurisdictions other than China. We are working cooperatively and actively negotiating with SAMR to secure China regulatory clearance, and we continue to anticipate closing in the first half of this year."

Ansys was down less than 1% on Thursday. Cadence had slid 1.6% and Keysight was relatively static.

Synopsys had not received the letter from the BIS when it presented its earnings on Wednesday evening.

"So I want to put this upfront as we go through the questions and given the news happened in the last few hours, just to acknowledge that we have not received the letter as well as our guidance that we shared with you reflects the current understanding of BIS export control," Ghazi said.

"So our guidance that we are reiterating for the full year reflects our current understanding of BIS export restrictions as well as our expectations for a year-over-year decline in China," he added.

The BIS implemented export controls on the most sophisticated chip making technology sales to China back in 2022. The extent of the new regulations are unclear at this time.

data.title: Ex-Goldman banker gets two-year sentence for 1MDB role - report
data.description:
Former Goldman Sachs (GS) banker Tim Leissner, who pleaded guilty for his role in stealing from Malaysian investment fund 1MDB, received a two-year prison sentence, according to media reports.

While he was given consideration for his cooperation with prosecutors, U.S. District Judge Margo Brodie in Brooklyn, New York, refused Leissner's request for no prison time, calling his conduct "brazen and audacious."

Leissner could have been sentenced as many as 25 years after pleading guilty in 2018 to one count of conspiring to violated bribery laws and conspiring to launder money. Even though he cooperated with prosecutors, his role in the scheme was apparently too pivotal to ignore. "Without you, it's unclear these crimes would have taken place," Brodie said, citing a letter from Goldman, as reported by Bloomberg News.

In 2020, Goldman Sachs (GS) agreed to pay more than $5B to settle global investigations related to 1MDB. The Wall Street bank helped raise $6.5B of capital for 1MDB through three bond transactions in 2012 and 2013. Eventually, it was discovered that billions of dollars were stolen from the fund.

In 2023, another former Goldman executive, Ng Chong Hwa, also known as Roger Ng, was sentenced to 10 years in prison for bribery and laundering funds embezzled from 1MDB.

data.title: TruGolf announces stock repurchase program
data.description:
• The board of directors of TruGolf Holdings (NASDAQ:TRUG) announced on Thursday the authorization of a stock repurchase program of up to $2 million of TruGolf’s class A common stock.
  • The stock repurchase program will expire upon the expenditure of $2 million, when terminated or otherwise completed.
  • Purchases may be made in open-market transactions or by other means as determined by TruGolf’s management and in accordance with the regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission.
  • “TruGolf’s capital position provides the opportunity to execute this stock repurchase program, which we believe further strengthens our shareholder value proposition,” said Chris Jones, CEO of TruGolf.
  • TRUG is +32.58% to $0.2775.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Sound Point Meridian Capital outlines $0.25 per share monthly distribution for Q3 2025 as CLO market volatility reshapes investment strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sound Point Meridian Capital Inc (SPMC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ujjaval Desai reported that for the quarter, SPMC generated net investment income of $13.4 million or $0.66 per share and net realized loss on exited investments of $0.08 per share, while dividends of $0.72 per share were paid. Net asset value per share fell to $18.78 from $20.52, primarily due to unrealized losses linked to uncertainty around the new administration’s tariff rollout and a reduction in government spending.
  • Desai highlighted, “During the quarter, we deployed approximately $70.6 million in six CLO warehouse investments. We purchased four CLO equity investments in the primary market with an amortized cost and weighted average GAAP yield of $16.9 million and 16.15%, respectively.”
  • The company refinanced the liabilities of three CLO equity investments and had one outstanding warehouse investment, plus one unfunded commitment with a cost of $12.3 million. For the year, $291.8 million was deployed into CLO equity investments across 17 new issue transactions, 19 refinancing transactions, and eight secondary market purchases.
  • Desai stated, “As of March 31, the weighted average GAAP yield on our CLO equity portfolio was 14.0% versus 15.2% as of December 31,” attributing the decrease to loan repricings in the underlying CLO portfolios.
  • The CEO also noted the portfolio’s diversification across 75 CLO investments managed by 23 CLO managers, totaling over 1,500 loan issuers across more than 30 industries.
  • Desai described new liquidity moves, including entering a $100 million revolving credit facility at SOFR plus 3.75% and issuing a $57.5 million Series A preferred offering at an 8% rate. The company also commenced a committed equity financing agreement with B. Riley Principal Capital II LLC, allowing for purchases of up to 4 million shares over 36 months.
  • CFO Kevin Gerlitz stated, “For the quarter ended March 31, 2025, we delivered net investment income of $13.4 million or $0.66 per share. For the quarter ended March 31, we recorded net realized losses of $1.7 million and unrealized losses on investments of $32.3 million.”
  • Gerlitz added, “GAAP net income loss for the quarter was $20.7 million or a loss of $1.02 per share.”
  • Gerlitz reported total assets of $514.1 million, net assets of $381.6 million, net asset value at $18.78 per share, and available liquidity of $9.9 million. Outstanding debt totaled 24.5% of total assets.

Outlook

  • Desai announced monthly distributions for Q3 2025 of $0.25 per share, unchanged from Q2 2025. As of April 30, estimated net asset value per share was $17.55.
  • Desai stated, “We believe our portfolio is defensively positioned in investments with longer reinvestment periods, allowing CLO managers to actively manage the underlying loan portfolios to avoid defaults and buy loans at discounted prices.”

Financial Results

  • SPMC reported net investment income of $13.4 million or $0.66 per share and net realized losses of $1.7 million. There were unrealized losses on investments of $32.3 million, and GAAP net income loss for the quarter was $20.7 million or $(1.02) per share.
  • The company declared monthly income distributions of $0.25 per share. The annualized dividend yield was 15% based on the share price as of March 31.
  • Total expenses were $9.3 million. The fair value of the investment portfolio was $503.7 million.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter ended March 31 was $16.6 million.

Q&A

  • Erik Zwick, Lucid Capital Markets: Asked about opportunities for refis and resets in the portfolio and deployment between primary and secondary markets. Ujjaval Desai explained that reset activity slowed in March and April due to volatility but is picking up as liability spreads tighten. “We have quite a few positions...which will be up for a reset this year and early next year. And so we expect to take advantage of that and that should certainly be very helpful to our portfolio.” Desai described being “very focused on trading our portfolio, reacting to the market,” with recent sales at tight levels and subsequent reinvestment in both primary and secondary opportunities.
  • Zwick asked about unrealized losses. Desai attributed them to “spread compression in the underlying loan portfolios through refinancings” and “general market wall (ph),” noting, “We haven’t seen any material increase in defaults or stress in our portfolios.”
  • Zwick inquired about recurring cash flows for the quarter. Kevin Gerlitz responded, “For quarter ending March 31, it was $16.6 million, that's for the full quarter.”
  • Tim D’Agostino, B. Riley Securities: Asked about the portfolio’s underweight exposure to healthcare. Desai explained, “We are selecting managers that are taking that view themselves,” and emphasized idiosyncratic, name-by-name analysis over broad sector calls.
  • D’Agostino asked about share issuance. Desai replied, “Not in Q1. There was no issuance of the preferred. We obviously have the B. Riley facility, which we had a small I think I mentioned $113,000 was raised in March.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on market volatility, unrealized losses, and sector positioning, expressing a slightly cautious tone, particularly around portfolio performance and risk management.
  • Management demonstrated a defensive but confident tone, frequently referencing active portfolio management and risk controls. Desai stated, “We feel that most of the drop we have seen is because of general market volatility and certainly we will – we expect that to recover and we've already seen market sentiment improve significantly in May.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone has shifted from generally positive to slightly more probing on risk factors. Management’s tone has grown more cautious, reflecting increased market uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance on monthly distributions remained unchanged at $0.25 per share for Q3 2025, after a 4.2% increase the prior quarter.
  • Net asset value per share declined to $18.78 from $20.52, while the previous quarter saw an increase.
  • GAAP net income shifted from $32.2 million in Q3 to a loss of $20.7 million in Q4, mainly due to unrealized losses.
  • Management’s tone has moved from bullish about market opportunities to more defensive, emphasizing downside protection and active risk management. Analysts’ questions have become more focused on volatility, unrealized losses, and sector-specific risks, compared to prior interest in market growth and credit quality.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward navigating market volatility, with greater emphasis on liquidity, portfolio repositioning, and seizing opportunities in both primary and secondary CLO markets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited market volatility due to the new administration’s tariffs and reduction in government spending as primary drivers of unrealized losses and net asset value decline.
  • The company is focused on defensive positioning with investments in CLOs with longer reinvestment periods to mitigate default risks and capture opportunistic loan purchases at discounted prices.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about sector exposures, recurring cash flow, and the impact of market-driven losses, prompting management to emphasize active trading and portfolio rotation.

Final Takeaway

SPMC’s fourth quarter reflected the impact of heightened market volatility, resulting in a net asset value decline and a shift from net income to a net loss, mainly due to unrealized losses. Management maintains a defensive portfolio stance with continued emphasis on active trading and risk management, while reaffirming its monthly distribution guidance at $0.25 per share for Q3 2025. The team remains focused on leveraging market dislocations for opportunistic deployment and believes the current positioning supports dividend sustainability and downside protection as volatility persists.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Li Auto rallies as investors eye resilient margins, new model introductions
data.description:
Li Auto's (NASDAQ:LI) Q1 earnings report released early on Thursday showed modest revenue growth and strong delivery gains compared to the same period last year. Total revenue was up 1.1% year-over-year to RMB25.9 billion, slightly ahead of analyst expectations and the company’s own guidance. The growth in revenue was driven by higher vehicle deliveries, which were up 15.5% to 92,864 units amid the seasonally slow period for auto sales in China. 

The Chinese electric vehicle maker's gross margin for the quarter was 20.5%, essentially flat compared to the same period a year ago, up slightly improved from the prior quarter. The vehicle margin edged up to 19.8% from 19.3% a year earlier, indicating a modest improvement in operational efficiency and cost management. Operating margin also improved to 1.0% from negative 2.3% in the first quarter of 2024, although it was lower than the previous quarter’s 8.4% due to seasonal effect and pricing pressure. Still, Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) outperformed many of its peers on the margin line.

Looking ahead, Li Auto (LI) plans to launch the i8 model in July and the i6 model in September.

On Wall Street, Bank of America reiterated its Buy rating on Li Auto (LI) as it factored in the company’s well-managed gross margin rate and strong free cash flow. Morgan Stanley also stayed bullish as it pointed to Li Auto's (LI) resilient margin rate.

Shares of Li Auto (LI) were up 3.6% in afternoon trading.

data.title: IQSTEL announces 51% acquisition of fintech GlobeTopper
data.description:
Telecommunication services provider IQSTEL (NASDAQ:IQST) announced on Thursday a 51% acquisition of the fintech GlobeTopper, with the transaction valued at up to $700K.

The definitive agreement comes as IQSTEL strives to become a $1B revenue corporation.

The transaction value includes $200K in staged cash payments and $500K in restricted IQSTEL common stock. Additionally, the acquisition includes performance-based earn-outs.

The transaction becomes effective July 1.

Craig Span is set to continue in his role as CEO of GlobeTopper.

data.title: Caleres suspends guidance amid tariff volatility and $15M SG&A cost reduction initiative
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Caleres (CAL) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Jay Schmidt, President and CEO, reported that "our first quarter results fell short of expectations. February sales were particularly weak, and although trends improved in March and in April, overall performance was below plan. Furthermore, operating earnings were pressured by lower gross margins, increased reserves and cost to cancel and move inventory." He noted that the operating environment had become more challenging, prompting the company to focus on optimizing sourcing strategy and reducing SG&A by $15 million annually through structural expense cuts.
  • Schmidt detailed impacts from the executive order on tariffs in April, stating, "we acted quickly to pause our production in China... each of these decisions had associated costs that impacted the quarter." He also highlighted brand performance, with Sam Edelman showing domestic and double-digit international sales growth and Famous Footwear launching Jordan in 147 stores as an exclusive in the Shoe Chain channel.
  • "For the first quarter, sales were $614.2 million, down 6.8%. This was below our expectations for sales, down 5% to 6%," stated CFO Jack Calandra. He added, "consolidated gross margin was 45.4%, down 150 basis points to last year and was driven by lower margins in both segments."

Outlook

  • Calandra announced, "given the continued uncertainty, we are suspending guidance at this time." He provided that Famous Footwear's quarter-to-date comparable sales were "slightly better than 1Q's, down 4.6%." Direct-to-consumer sales in the brand portfolio increased modestly year over year. Management expects the $15 million SG&A reduction to begin in Q3, with $7.5 million savings realized in the back half of 2025.
  • Management described the sourcing environment as "fluid" and intends to remain "nimble," with only 10% of sourcing dollars expected from China in the back half of the year.

Financial Results

  • Caleres reported Q1 adjusted earnings per share of $0.22 and sales of $614.2 million. Brand portfolio sales declined 6.9%. Tariff-related challenges and discrete items reduced sales by about $7 million compared to last year. Famous Footwear sales declined 6.3% and comparable sales were down 4.6%. Consolidated gross margin fell to 45.4%, driven by lower segment margins and increased reserves. Operating earnings were $12.2 million and operating margin was 2%.
  • Inventory ended at $573.6 million, up $43 million or 8.1% from last year, with the increase coming from both Famous and the brand portfolio. Cash flow from operations was negative $5.7 million, attributed to lower net income and higher inventory levels.

Q&A

  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about price increases, gross margin outlook, potential restructuring or brand exits, back-to-school inventory, and SG&A cuts. Schmidt stated that price increases are "brand by brand" and will begin with fall receipts. He said, "we don't have anything to announce" on restructuring but will continue to review. On back-to-school, "so far, we're in a pretty good position" and Famous will have 53 FLAIR stores for the season. Calandra clarified that "these reductions are permanent and structural in nature" and $7.5 million savings will be realized evenly in Q3 and Q4.
  • Laura Champine, Loop Capital Markets: Questioned the impact of the Jordan launch and tariff changes. Calandra responded that the Jordan launch was "very new" with "fairly limited marketing at this point" but the company is "very excited about the potential." Schmidt emphasized no immediate change in China sourcing despite tariff news, describing the environment as "fluid."
  • Mitch Kummetz, Seaport Research: Sought details on the Jordan launch's impact and cost pressures. Schmidt did not provide specific lift data but highlighted strong early sell-through, especially in men's and boys. On tariffs, Schmidt said, "it's very new and I think we're still... trying to get as many answers to it right now." Calandra broke down cost impacts: inventory markdown reserves for spring 2025 are believed to be well-covered, while tariff-related costs remain "TBD."
  • Chris Broszell (for Ashley Owens), KeyBanc: Asked about the persistence of operating earnings pressures. Schmidt indicated that inventory reserves are not expected to be a continuing headwind, while tariff-related impacts are unpredictable. Price increases are expected to be a smaller contributor to cost offset.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about inventory levels, the impact of tariffs, and margin pressure, with a generally cautious tone. Telsey's multi-part questions and Champine's focus on trend shifts and tariffs reflected skepticism about near-term recovery.
  • Management maintained a defensive stance, repeatedly describing the environment as "fluid" and emphasizing efforts to optimize costs and sourcing. Schmidt and Calandra used hedging language such as "we believe," "we are confident," and "we will remain nimble."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from cautious optimism about new initiatives to deeper concern about operational challenges. Management's tone became more defensive and focused on immediate mitigation, a change from the prior quarter's emphasis on strategic investments and growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change was the suspension of company guidance amid tariff uncertainty, compared to the previous quarter's issuance of full-year and Q1 guidance. In Q4 2024, management expressed confidence in sequential improvement and detailed multiple growth drivers, while in Q1 2025, the focus shifted to cost containment and risk management.
  • Key metrics deteriorated: Q4 adjusted EPS was $0.33, while Q1 2025 was $0.22; Q4 sales were $639.2 million vs. Q1's $614.2 million. Inventory increased from $565 million to $573.6 million.
  • Analysts in the previous quarter probed for upside in contemporary and international brands; in the current quarter, their focus shifted to risk, inventory, tariffs, and cost structure.
  • Management's confidence in growth initiatives, such as FLAIR stores and brand launches, was tempered by the suspension of guidance and emphasis on cost controls.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited challenges from tariff escalation, sourcing disruptions, increased inventory levels, and worsening customer credit issues. They noted the impact of tariffs as "fluid" and unpredictable and are monitoring the situation for further actions.
  • Calandra explained that strategies to offset costs include "factory concessions, lower gross margin and select price increases." Inventory risks remain, but management believes reserves are sufficient for spring 2025 product.
  • Analysts raised concerns about margin sustainability, brand performance variability, and the timing and effectiveness of cost reductions.

Final Takeaway

Caleres management acknowledged a challenging quarter with underperformance in sales and margins, driven by tariff-related disruptions, elevated inventories, and customer credit concerns. The company has suspended forward guidance due to ongoing volatility but is executing a $15 million annualized SG&A reduction to offset pressures. While management remains optimistic about long-term opportunities, including the integration of Stuart Weitzman and growth from FLAIR stores and brand launches, the near-term focus is on cost control, sourcing agility, and navigating a fluid external environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Build-A-Bear updates 2025 pre-tax guidance to $61M-$67M while expanding global footprint
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (BBW) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Sharon Price John, President and CEO, highlighted, "we have reported the best first quarter results in Build-A-Bear’s history. Double-digit top-line growth drove record revenue, margin expansion, and strong performance across all segments. This represents impressive earnings per share growth of nearly 43%." She cited the company’s ongoing strategy of expanding new product categories, reaching the over-18 kidult segment, and growing international presence, now in 30% of countries. John stated, "Revenue increased 11.9% to more than $128 million. Pre-tax income grew 30.6% to nearly $20 million, and EPS increased 42.7% to $1.17. Additionally, we returned over $7 million in capital to shareholders." John reiterated 2025 revenue guidance, with an update to pre-tax guidance due to tariffs.
  • Christopher Hurt, Chief Operations and Experience Officer, described global footprint expansion: "We opened 15 net new experience locations in the first quarter, highlighted by the opening of our corporately-operated Dublin, Ireland location... As we continue to grow the brand in more places, we’ve added two more countries, bringing our total count to 30%." Hurt confirmed expansion plans remain on track, expecting at least 50 net new locations for 2025.
  • Voin Todorovic, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "This was the most profitable first quarter in the company’s history. We grew across all segments, expanded the gross profit margin, and increased pre-tax income to a new record. This reflects the work from our strategic initiatives over the past several years and the business operating at a higher level of profitability."

Outlook

  • The company maintained its 2025 revenue guidance and continues to expect the addition of at least 50 net new experience locations, mostly operated by international partners. Commercial segment revenue is expected to grow at least 20% for the year. The company updated pre-tax income guidance to a range of $61 million to $67 million, inclusive of current tariff rates. Todorovic said, "Given Build-A-Bear’s solid business fundamentals and assuming tariffs remain at current levels, we expect to report record revenue for the year and maintain strong profitability with double-digit pre-tax margins."

Financial Results

  • Build-A-Bear reported total revenues of $128.4 million for the quarter, an increase of 11.9%. Net retail sales were $119.6 million, up 10.9%. All four store levers—traffic, conversion, average unit retail, and units per transaction—were positive. Domestic store traffic rose 3%, while national U.S. traffic declined by 3%. E-commerce demand increased 0.5%. Commercial revenue, including international franchise revenue, grew 28.3%. Gross margin reached 56.8%, a 260 basis point improvement. SG&A expenses were $53.7 million or 41.7% of revenues. Pre-tax income reached $19.6 million, representing 15.3% of revenues. EPS was $1.17. Cash balance at quarter end was $44.3 million, after returning $37 million to shareholders over 12 months. Inventory was $72.3 million, attributed to accelerated core product purchases and nearly $2 million in tariff costs.

Q&A

  • Eric Beder, SCC Research: Asked about leveraging movie tie-ins and flexibility to respond to pop culture trends. Sharon Price John explained the company maintains strong ties with major film creators like Disney and is prepared to capitalize on such events, but now views them as incremental rather than core to the business. She also detailed improvements in inventory management systems, enabling faster, more accurate responses to social media trends, saying, "We’re much more flexible now... will increase our confidence in even going longer on some of these things that we think are going to be hot."
  • Keegan Cox, D.A. Davidson: Asked about retail segment performance and partner-operated store strategy. Voin Todorovic responded, "All four levers were positive. Traffic was about 3%... higher traffic, we were able to increase our conversion, and... drive units per transaction, as well as with lower discounts, our average unit retail has gone up." Sharon Price John and Christopher Hurt described the high standards and careful selection process for international partners.
  • Greg Gibas, Northland Securities: Inquired about Mini Beans expansion and updated outlook for tariffs. Sharon Price John said Mini Beans have been introduced in retailers outside workshops and domestically in select locations. Christopher Hurt added they are now in 50 Applegreen stores and several Hudson airport locations. Voin Todorovic clarified, "our new pre-tax range, now $61 million to $67 million. This range reflects less than $10 million net of mitigation impact of tariffs and associated costs."
  • Steve Silver, Argus Research: Asked about international expansion and in-store traffic trends. Sharon Price John described the value of working with local market experts and the enthusiasm demonstrated by international partners. On Q1 traffic, she stated, "between 60% and 80% of trips that are made to Build-A-Bear are planned in advance," reinforcing Build-A-Bear's status as a destination retailer.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on tariff mitigation, partner strategy, product expansion, and inventory flexibility, reflecting a slightly positive to neutral tone as they sought insights on growth and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, especially in prepared remarks. Sharon Price John said, "We believe we are well positioned to manage an environment where uncertainty has become the norm rather than the exception." In Q&A, management responses were detailed and reinforced confidence in their strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains confident but slightly more focused on cost pressures from tariffs. Analyst questions in both quarters probed for risk management and growth levers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth accelerated to 11.9% in Q1 2025 from a full-year increase of 3.6% in FY 2024. Gross margin improved from 56.6% in Q4 to 56.8% in Q1. Q1 pre-tax income margin was 15.3%, compared to 18.3% for Q4. Management reiterated guidance for at least 50 net new experience locations in 2025 and updated pre-tax income guidance to $61 million-$67 million, versus previous commentary noting caution due to tariffs. The tone continued to emphasize resilience and strategy execution, but with greater detail on tariff mitigation. Analysts continued to focus on store performance, product expansion, and risk management, with similar sentiment as prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing tariff costs, with an expected impact of less than $10 million for 2025 after mitigation efforts. Additional headwinds include $5 million in medical and labor costs. Todorovic outlined sourcing diversification, reduced reliance on China, and the ability to redirect China-sourced products to international locations as key mitigation strategies. Inventory was increased to manage tariff risk. Analysts raised questions around the sustainability of growth, partner quality, and flexibility in inventory management.

Final Takeaway

Build-A-Bear Workshop delivered its strongest first quarter ever, driven by double-digit revenue growth, robust retail and commercial segment performance, and ongoing global expansion. Management reaffirmed revenue guidance for 2025 and updated pre-tax income expectations in response to tariffs, while highlighting successful product innovation and diversification strategies. With a record-setting start to the year and solid momentum into the second quarter, the company remains focused on executing its strategic initiatives, expanding its international presence, and maintaining strong profitability in a dynamic environment.
